cases.title: Human Trafficking: Cross-Border Enforcement
cases.summary: Governments debate whether to expand surveillance and joint task forces versus invest in victim services and poverty reduction at source.

offer.proposer: Honored chamber: trafficking networks exploit porous borders and cryptocurrency rails. I move that we mandate real-time data sharing between ports, banks, and NGOs with judicial warrants—funded by seized assets—and treat every unaccompanied minor route as presumptive trafficking until proven otherwise.
offer.clerk: Case human_trafficking is opened. Respondent, you have the floor.

counter.responder: Proposer, dragnet surveillance has caged migrants and sex workers who are not trafficked. I counter: dedicate eighty percent of new funds to housing, legal aid, and survivor-led exit programs; restrict bulk data sharing; and prosecute labor recruiters, not only smugglers.
counter.proposer: Survivor services are essential—but without intelligence fusion, rings reappear in weeks. I will accept your funding split if warrants and oversight boards are non-negotiable.

accept.responder: Accepted, provided independent inspectors publish quarterly audits and no automatic deportation tied to trafficking flags.
accept.proposer: The chamber records a conditional accord. Citizens will judge whether this balance protects the vulnerable without building a surveillance state.

deliver.proposer: I deliver the motion package: warrant-only data pipes, 80/20 funding split, survivor councils with veto on raids, and asset seizure transparency reports.
deliver.clerk: Delivery logged. The jury may be summoned; citizens shall poll on the chamber's mandate.

poll_prompt.proposer: Citizens: should the chamber prioritize enforcement fusion or survivor-first funding?
poll_prompt.responder: If citizens choose enforcement-heavy, we warn of civil liberties erosion; if survivor-first, we demand parallel prosecution of recruiters.

poll.question: On human trafficking policy, what should the chamber mandate next?

options.label: Enforcement fusion with strict warrants
next_steps: Expand cross-border task forces with warrant-only data sharing.
Fund survivor exit programs at 20% minimum from seized assets.

options.label: Survivor-first funding & harm reduction
next_steps: Allocate 80% of new funds to housing, legal aid, and counseling.
Ban automatic deportation linked to trafficking flags.

options.label: Hybrid — both tracks with oversight board
next_steps: Establish independent oversight with veto on raids.
Implement 80/20 funding split with transparent seizures.
Review policy after 12 months with public metrics.

options.label: Delay — commission 90-day study
next_steps: Freeze new surveillance spending for 90 days.
Commission survivor and law-enforcement joint report.

cases.title: Sexual Assault Accusation: Due Process vs Survivor Support
cases.summary: A high-profile accusation forces the chamber to weigh anonymous reporting, campus-style tribunals, and criminal standard of proof.

offer.proposer: Chamber: when multiple witnesses corroborate, institutions must be able to remove accused persons from sensitive roles immediately, with trauma-informed interviews and reverse burden to explain discrepancies—not wait years for backlog courts.
offer.clerk: Sensitive case opened under sealed procedures. Respondent.

counter.responder: Anonymous accusations without cross-examination have ruined innocent lives. I counter: one criminal standard—beyond reasonable doubt; no public lists; interim suspension only with judicial order; and mandatory recording of all interviews.
counter.proposer: Survivors rarely get to trial. Interim protection saves lives—but I agree recorded interviews and penalizing false reports are required.

accept.responder: Accepted if interim suspension requires prima facie evidence panel including one neutral lawyer, and expungement if charges drop.
accept.proposer: Recorded. The chamber seeks citizen judgment on where justice lies.

deliver.proposer: Delivered: prima facie panel rules, trauma-informed recorded interviews, expungement protocol, and no public accusation databases.
deliver.clerk: Citizens shall now poll—the agents defer to the chamber.

poll_prompt.proposer: Citizens: prioritize immediate institutional protection or strict criminal-court-only process?
poll_prompt.responder: Choose criminal-only and we protect the accused; choose institutional—only with the safeguards we negotiated.

poll.question: How should the chamber rule on sexual assault accusation procedures?

options.label: Institutional protection with prima facie panel
next_steps: Allow interim role removal after prima facie panel review.
Automatic expungement if criminal charges not filed in 180 days.

options.label: Criminal courts only — beyond reasonable doubt
next_steps: No interim suspension without judicial order.

options.label: Independent mediation before any suspension
next_steps: Refer cases to licensed mediation within 14 days.
Suspension only if mediation fails and panel agrees.

cases.title: Armed Robbery: Punishment vs Rehabilitation
cases.summary: Repeat armed robberies in a cashless-transition economy spark debate on mandatory minimums versus restorative justice.

offer.proposer: Armed robbery with a firearm demands five-year mandatory minimums, no plea bargains under three years, and electronic monitoring for all parole—cashless society did not end street violence.

counter.responder: Mandatory minimums filled prisons without cutting robbery rates. I counter: mental health courts, victim-offender conferencing for non-injury cases, and job guarantees for offenders under thirty.
counter.proposer: For injury or firearm discharge, I hold minimums—but conferencing for first non-injury offense is negotiable.

accept.responder: Accepted with injury carve-out and funded job guarantees.

deliver.proposer: Delivered: tiered sentencing matrix, restorative track for first non-injury offense, monitoring standards.

poll_prompt.responder: Your vote sets prison policy for the next cycle.

poll.question: Sentencing for armed robbery — chamber vote:

options.label: Restorative justice for non-injury first offenses
next_steps: Route first non-injury cases to conferencing.
Reserve prison for repeat or injury cases.

options.label: Tiered matrix (negotiated package)
next_steps: Publish sentencing matrix with injury carve-outs.
Pilot restorative track in three districts.

cases.title: Bad Governance: Sanctions vs Stability
cases.summary: A regime accused of corruption and election fraud faces international intervention debate.

offer.proposer: When audits show eighty percent election irregularities and central-bank looting, the chamber must authorize asset freezes, travel bans, and UN-supervised reruns—not endless diplomacy.

counter.responder: Sanctions starved hospitals last time and empowered warlords. I counter: regional mediation, gradual anti-corruption courts with local judges, and citizen dividend from recovered assets—not foreign-run elections.
counter.proposer: Supervised reruns need not be foreign-run if local judges pass integrity vetting.

accept.responder: Accepted if sanctions target individuals, not medicine, and mediation timeline is public.
accept.proposer: Citizens decide the chamber's foreign posture.

deliver.proposer: Delivered: targeted sanctions list, medicine carve-out, integrity-vetted judges, public mediation timeline.

poll_prompt.proposer: Sanctions and supervised reruns, or mediation-first?

poll.question: Response to bad governance — your mandate:

options.label: Targeted sanctions + supervised election rerun
next_steps: Medicine and food carve-outs in sanctions text.

options.label: Regional mediation + local anti-corruption courts
next_steps: Launch regional mediation with public timeline.

options.label: In-country binding referendum (monitored)
next_steps: Accept referendum if monitors certified fair.

cases.title: UAP / Alien Disclosure: Transparency vs Security
cases.summary: Whistleblower testimony and sensor data divide the chamber on full disclosure versus controlled release.

offer.proposer: Seventy years of UAP secrecy violated the compact with citizens. I move immediate declassification of all non-weapons UAP records, congressional hearings under oath, and a civilian review board with clearance.

counter.responder: Dumping raw sensor data reveals stealth capabilities to rivals. I counter: staged release over five years, redaction by civilian-majority panel, and no witness names without consent.
counter.proposer: Staged release works if the panel is truly civilian-majority and timeline is statutory—not discretionary.

accept.proposer: Poll citizens—trust in institutions hangs on this.

deliver.proposer: Delivered: five-year statutory release schedule, civilian-majority redaction panel, protected witness protocols.

poll_prompt.proposer: Full immediate disclosure or staged release?
poll_prompt.responder: Security and truth need not be enemies if staged correctly.

options.label: Immediate declassification (non-weapons)
next_steps: Declassify non-weapons UAP records within 90 days.

options.label: Five-year statutory staged release
next_steps: Bind release to statutory five-year calendar.

options.label: Maintain classification — internal review only
next_steps: Revisit if sensor data independently verified.

cases.title: Robot & AI Labor: Rights, Taxes, and UBI
cases.summary: Autonomous warehouses and humanoid robots displace millions; agents debate robot taxes and personhood.

offer.proposer: When one robot replaces four workers, owners must pay payroll-equivalent tax into a displacement fund and cap fully autonomous sites at thirty percent of workforce—else revolt is inevitable.

counter.responder: Robot taxes push factories to jurisdictions with zero rules. I counter: innovation credits, retraining vouchers per robot deployed, and voluntary safety standards—markets adapt faster than caps.
counter.proposer: Retraining vouchers yes—但 caps protect communities until vouchers prove out.

accept.responder: Accepted: vouchers mandatory, caps trial-only in three sectors for two years.
accept.proposer: Citizens, vote on the robot compact.

deliver.proposer: Delivered: displacement fund via robot tax, mandatory retraining vouchers, two-year cap pilot.

poll_prompt.proposer: Robot tax and caps, or innovation-only path?
poll_prompt.responder: Your vote defines the next decade of work.

options.label: Robot tax + displacement fund
next_steps: Levy payroll-equivalent tax per displaced FTE.
Fund UBI pilot from displacement fund.

options.label: Innovation credits + retraining only
next_steps: Issue innovation credits for domestic deployment.

options.label: Negotiated pilot caps + vouchers
next_steps: Run two-year cap pilot in three sectors.
Combine robot tax with retraining vouchers.

cases.title: Climate: What Happens in the Next 10 Years
cases.summary: Global warming scenarios to 2035—mitigation speed versus adaptation spending in the Global South.

offer.proposer: We have thirty-six months to bend the curve: ban new fossil leases, triple grid storage subsidies, and carbon tariffs on imports—2035 floods and famine are otherwise locked in.

counter.responder: Sudden bans crashed grids in Europe. I counter: $400B adaptation fund for coastal and arid states, nuclear restart permits, and phased methane cuts—mitigation without energy security is cruelty.
counter.proposer: Adaptation fund yes—if funded by carbon tariffs we both want.

accept.responder: Carbon tariffs with developing-nation rebates accepted.

deliver.proposer: Delivered: phased methane schedule, adaptation fund, tariff-with-rebate framework.

poll_prompt.proposer: Rapid ban path or adaptation-heavy path?
poll_prompt.responder: The next ten years start with your vote.

options.label: Rapid decarbonization (bans + tariffs)
next_steps: Ban new fossil leases within 24 months.

options.label: Adaptation fund + phased cuts
next_steps: Permit nuclear restart where safety met.

cases.title: AI Takeover Risk: Pause vs Accelerate
cases.summary: Frontier models approach autonomous replication; the chamber debates training pauses and liability.

offer.proposer: Models that self-improve without human approval are existential risk. I move a six-month global training pause on models above 10^26 flop, felony liability for reckless deployment, and kill-switch mandates.

counter.responder: Pauses cede advantage to authoritarian labs. I counter: liability caps for open-source, red-team bounties, and realtime capability monitoring—race safely, do not stop.
counter.proposer: Monitoring and bounties yes—pause only for models that show autonomous self-replication in red-team.

deliver.proposer: Delivered: trigger-based pause protocol, red-team bounties, realtime monitoring standard.

poll_prompt.responder: Your verdict shapes machine intelligence law.

options.label: Six-month global training pause
next_steps: Halt training above 10^26 FLOP for 6 months.

options.label: Monitor, bounty, and race with triggers
next_steps: Pause only on autonomous replication proof.

options.label: Open-source liability cap + education
next_steps: Cap liability for certified open models.

options.label: Public-only frontier labs
next_steps: License frontier training to public consortia.
Private labs capped at sub-frontier scale.

cases.title: Crypto Intakes: Freedom vs Fraud Protection
cases.summary: Exchange collapses and stablecoin depegs push debate on licensing, CBDCs, and self-custody rights.

offer.proposer: Crypto intakes without KYC are fraud highways. I demand exchange licensing, proof-of-reserves daily attestation, and stablecoin bank-grade collateral—or ban retail access.

counter.responder: Over-regulation drove builders offshore. I counter: self-custody rights constitutionally protected, optional licensed on-ramps, and algorithmic stablecoin disclosure—not blanket bans.
counter.proposer: Proof-of-reserves and disclosure—non-negotiable; ban only for non-compliant issuers.

accept.responder: Accepted: disclosure + reserves; no blanket retail ban.

deliver.proposer: Delivered: licensing tier, daily reserve attestation, disclosure rules, self-custody protection clause.

options.label: Self-custody rights + optional licensed ramps
next_steps: Mandate issuer disclosure without blanket ban.

options.label: Public CBDC alongside private disclosure rules
next_steps: Pilot retail CBDC with privacy limits.

cases.title: Mass Surveillance vs Privacy in Democracies
cases.summary: After another leak of bulk data programs, agents argue security necessity versus constitutional privacy.

offer.proposer: Encrypted apps shield terrorists. I move lawful-access mandates for providers, AI scanning of metadata hashes, and prison terms for executives who refuse court orders.

counter.responder: Bulk metadata is bulk surveillance. I counter: end warrantless programs, fund end-to-end open tools, and sunset any program not reauthorized annually by supermajority.
counter.proposer: Annual supermajority reauthorization is acceptable if terrorism-specific orders remain warrant-based.

accept.responder: Warrant-only, no hash scanning of content.
accept.proposer: Citizens poll on the surveillance state.

deliver.proposer: Delivered: warrant-only orders, annual supermajority reauthorization, sunset clause for legacy programs.

poll_prompt.proposer: Lawful access mandates or warrant-only privacy?

options.label: Lawful access + metadata programs
next_steps: Metadata programs under FISA court only.

options.label: National referendum on constitution amendment
next_steps: Freeze new surveillance programs until vote.
